prompt: While the market features diverse quantitative strategies like multi-factor and high-frequency trading, it lacks a single, standardized benchmark for assessing their performance across multiple dimensions such as returns, risk, and adaptability to market conditions. Could we develop a general yet rigorous evaluation framework to enable accurate comparison and analysis of various advanced quant strategies?
article:
Assumptions (kept minimal): “Advanced quant strategies” includes (i) medium/low‑frequency systematic alpha (multi‑factor, stat arb, trend/CTA), (ii) high‑frequency market making / execution‑alpha strategies, and (iii) ML/AI‑driven signals (incl. alternative data) that may adapt post‑deployment. The goal is a *general yet rigorous* framework that enables *apples‑to‑apples comparison* while still being faithful to each strategy’s mechanics and constraints.

---

## 1) Why there is no single benchmark today (and what “standardized” must mean)

A single scalar metric (e.g., Sharpe) cannot be a universal yardstick because strategies differ along at least five axes:

1) **Return formation** (carry vs momentum vs microstructure rebates vs prediction alpha).  
2) **Path dependence and tail shape** (e.g., crash risk, drawdowns). Maximum drawdown is explicitly *cumulative and path‑dependent* and captures loss experiences that single‑period volatility cannot [[23]](https://ryanoconnellfinance.com/maximum-drawdown/).  
3) **Horizon / autocorrelation / smoothing** (HFT seconds vs monthly). High autocorrelation can coincide with “extraordinary mean returns and Sharpe ratios,” implying naive Sharpe comparisons can mislead when serial dependence differs [[28]](http://www.efmaefm.org/0EFMAMEETINGS/EFMA%20ANNUAL%20MEETINGS/2024-Lisbon/papers/OFM.pdf).  
4) **Capacity and tradability** (costs, impact, liquidity).  
5) **Non‑stationarity & selection bias**: modern research can test millions/billions of variants, making false discoveries likely unless the *number of trials* and multiple‑testing effects are controlled [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf), [[54]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/backtest-prob.pdf).

So “standardization” cannot mean “one number.” It must mean:
- A **standard evaluation protocol** (data handling, backtest design, costs, leakage controls, overfitting diagnostics, reporting).
- A **standard metric dictionary** (precise definitions, horizons, sign conventions).
- A **standard benchmark stack** (what counts as “beta,” “style premia,” “execution alpha,” etc.).
- A **standard disclosure & governance package** (auditability, model inventory/versioning, validation evidence) aligned with existing model risk management expectations [[64]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/sr1107.htm).

---

## 2) A general evaluation architecture: a layered “Quant Strategy Evaluation Framework” (QSEF)

### Layer A — Strategy specification (what is being evaluated)
To compare heterogeneous strategies, the framework must force *explicit declarations*:

1) **Intent / mandate / objective** (e.g., absolute return, benchmark‑relative, market‑neutral).  
   GIPS explicitly treats benchmark selection as central to fair representation and distinguishes “benchmark relative,” “benchmark aware,” and “benchmark agnostic” strategies [[70]](https://www.gipsstandards.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gs_benchmarks_firms.pdf). A quant evaluation framework should adopt the same classification.

2) **Instruments & venues** (cash equities, futures, options, OTC; lit vs dark; exchange set).  
3) **Holding period distribution** (median/95%ile holding time; percent of P&L from overnight).  
4) **Leverage & financing assumptions** (margin model, haircuts, funding rate, borrow costs).  
5) **Execution style** (market/limit, passive/aggressive ratio, queue participation).  
6) **Capacity statement**: “strategy is scalable to X notional with Y slippage model,” with evidence (see Layer F).

This “strategy spec” becomes the top block of the standardized report and is the anchor for benchmark choice (Layer D) per GIPS “appropriate benchmark reflects mandate/objective/strategy” [[70]](https://www.gipsstandards.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gs_benchmarks_firms.pdf).

---

### Layer B — Data and backtest integrity (preventing biased comparisons)
Because “data snooping” arises whenever the same dataset is reused for inference/model selection [[39]](https://www.ssc.wisc.edu/~bhansen/718/White2000.pdf), the evaluation must standardize:

1) **Dataset provenance & point‑in‑time integrity** (survivorship‑free universes, timestamp integrity).  
2) **Train/validation/test separation and leakage controls** (especially for labeled events).  
   (In the gathered sources, a purging/embargo concept is described as preventing overlap between training observations and the label horizon of the test set [[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Purged_cross-validation)—note Wikipedia is secondary, but the principle aligns with the need to prevent leakage.)

3) **Trial accounting**: record *how many strategy variants were tried* and how the “winner” was selected.  
   Bailey & López de Prado argue the “most important” missing information in most backtests is the number of trials attempted; without it, backtests are not assessable [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf). The PBO paper similarly stresses backtests “almost never declare the number of trials” despite billions of tests making false positives near‑certain [[54]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/backtest-prob.pdf).

Deliverable: a **Backtest Lineage Card** (dataset IDs, time ranges, feature availability rules, labeling horizon, and trial counts) attached to every evaluation.

---

### Layer C — Metric suite (multi-dimensional, standardized definitions)
A universal framework should publish a **minimum required metric set** plus **strategy‑class add‑ons**.

#### C1) Minimum required metrics (all strategies)

**(i) Returns & aggregation**
- Time‑weighted return (TWR) vs money‑weighted return (MWR): GIPS requires benchmark reporting aligned to whether TWR or MWR is presented and requires benchmark returns for the same periods shown [[70]](https://www.gipsstandards.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gs_benchmarks_firms.pdf).  
- Report **gross** and **net** (fees + transaction costs + financing) and reconcile.

**(ii) Volatility & risk-adjusted return**
- Sharpe ratio traces to Sharpe’s classic mutual fund performance work [[4]](http://www.stat.ucla.edu/~nchristo/statistics_c183_c283/sharpe__mutual_fund_performance.pdf).  
- Report Sharpe at multiple horizons (daily, weekly, monthly) *and* report serial correlation diagnostics (because Sharpe can be inflated by smoothing/autocorrelation, consistent with evidence that high autocorrelation can coexist with very high Sharpe [[28]](http://www.efmaefm.org/0EFMAMEETINGS/EFMA%20ANNUAL%20MEETINGS/2024-Lisbon/papers/OFM.pdf)).

**(iii) Downside & tail risk**
- **VaR** must always specify horizon: VaR quantifies potential losses “over a specified timeframe” [[21]](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/v/var.asp). Standardization requires VaR(α, horizon, method).  
- **CVaR / Expected Shortfall**: defined as expected losses equal to or exceeding VaR (“Tail VaR”) [[22]](https://www.mathematik.hu-berlin.de/~romisch/SP01/Uryasev.pdf). Report ES alongside VaR.

**(iv) Drawdowns**
- **Maximum drawdown** should be mandatory because it captures “single worst loss experience” and is path‑dependent [[23]](https://ryanoconnellfinance.com/maximum-drawdown/).  
- Also report time‑under‑water/time‑to‑recovery (definition can be standardized even though not explicitly defined in the gathered sources).

**(v) Distribution shape**
- Skewness and kurtosis: required inputs for deflating Sharpe when returns are non‑normal [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf).

**(vi) Dependency / concentration**
- Autocorrelation (multiple lags).  
- Correlation to canonical benchmarks and to style premia indices (Layer D).

**(vii) Robustness / statistical credibility**
- **Deflated Sharpe Ratio (DSR)**: corrects Sharpe inflation from selection bias under multiple testing and non‑normal returns [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf). DSR explicitly incorporates skewness, kurtosis, sample length, dispersion of Sharpe across trials, and number of independent trials [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf).  
- **Probability of Backtest Overfitting (PBO)**: quantifies overfitting risk of the model selection process; CSCV provides a nonparametric procedure for estimating it [[54]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/backtest-prob.pdf).  
- **Reality Check / SPA family**: White’s “Reality Check” tests whether the *best* model found in a specification search has predictive superiority over a benchmark despite data snooping [[39]](https://www.ssc.wisc.edu/~bhansen/718/White2000.pdf). Subsequent work highlights RC conservatism and SPA/stepwise SPA approaches for more power while controlling familywise error [[55]](https://homepage.ntu.edu.tw/~ckuan/pdf/Step-SPA-20090720.pdf).

These three (DSR, PBO, RC/SPA) are complementary: DSR adjusts performance claims; PBO diagnoses selection process fragility; RC/SPA performs a joint hypothesis test against “luck.”

**(viii) Stress/scenario performance**
- A standardized macro scenario library can be borrowed from regulatory stress testing publications. The Federal Reserve’s 2025 scenarios provide a fully specified severe recession path, market volatility shock features, and a “global market shock” component for trading exposures [[93]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/publications/2025-stress-test-scenarios.htm). Even though the Fed scenarios are designed for banks, the *structure* (named variables, time paths, and explicit shock narratives) is suitable for standardized strategy stress evaluation.

#### C2) Strategy-class add-ons (to preserve fidelity)

**HFT / market making / execution‑alpha**
- Fill statistics, queue position metrics, adverse selection proxies, spread capture, inventory risk.  
- Latency sensitivity: Cartea–Jaimungal–Penalva explicitly include limit order book mechanics, trading costs, price impact, and “latency and tick size” topics, indicating these are core dimensions in algorithmic trading evaluation [[59]](https://assets.cambridge.org/97811070/91146/frontmatter/9781107091146_frontmatter.pdf).  
- Microstructure‑consistent slippage models: Hasbrouck emphasizes transaction price = efficient price + noise and the centrality of the limit order book [[60]](https://www.acsu.buffalo.edu/~keechung/MGF743/Readings/Hasbrouck's%20book.pdf).  
- Simulation: queue‑reactive LOB models can serve as realistic simulators and tools for transaction cost analysis of complex trading algorithms [[61]](https://arxiv.org/abs/1312.0563).

**Options / volatility strategies**
- Greeks exposure profiles, jump/crash sensitivity, stress to vol surface shocks.

**Medium-frequency factor / stat arb**
- Turnover, borrow utilization, crowding proxies, factor exposure stability.

**ML/AI adaptive strategies**
- Drift indicators, retraining frequency, feature stability, explainability artifacts (Layer G).

---

## 3) Benchmarking: a unified “benchmark stack” rather than a single benchmark

### D1) Benchmark selection rules (governance + comparability)
Borrow directly from GIPS benchmark principles:

A benchmark should be specified in advance, relevant, measurable, unambiguous, representative, investable, complete, and the firm must be accountable for deviations [[70]](https://www.gipsstandards.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gs_benchmarks_firms.pdf). Peer groups are explicitly cautioned due to self‑reporting bias, survivorship bias, lack of investability, and interpretability issues for risk statistics [[70]](https://www.gipsstandards.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gs_benchmarks_firms.pdf).

This is directly portable to quant strategy evaluation: a benchmark stack must be *pre-declared* and *investable enough* to reflect a realistic alternative.

### D2) The benchmark stack (four layers)
To compare across strategy classes, define performance at multiple residual layers:

1) **Layer 0: Cash / funding benchmark** (strategy’s base currency funding rate).  
2) **Layer 1: Market beta benchmark** (broad asset class index / futures).  
3) **Layer 2: Style / alternative risk premia benchmark** (carry, value, momentum, defensive, etc.).  
   - Asness et al. construct cross‑asset long–short style portfolios (Value, Momentum, Carry, Defensive), with explicit signal definitions, volatility targeting, and risk weighting across asset classes [[73]](https://www.aqr.com/-/media/AQR/Documents/Insights/Journal-Article/JOIM-Investing-With-Style.pdf). This provides a concrete template for building *canonical* style baselines usable across many quant strategies.  
   - The bfinance report describes the industry’s push for comprehensive ARP indices and notes Bloomberg and GSAM launched ARP benchmark suites, emphasizing simplicity, consistency, tradability, and consensus alignment rather than “best back-tests” [[74]](https://thehedgefundjournal.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/10/bfinance-Benchmarking-ARP-v1.pdf). This is directly aligned with the goal of standardized benchmarks.

4) **Layer 3: Strategy-family replicators** (especially for hedge-fund-like payoffs).  
   - Hedge fund replication can be framed as matching statistical properties of payoffs rather than only time-series tracking; the “Replication and Benchmarking of Hedge Funds” paper reports property‑matching replication can outperform regression replication on property‑error measures and explicitly discusses matching higher moments and factor covariances [[75]](http://faculty.washington.edu/yli2/Publications/Published_JAI_HedgeFundReplication.pdf).  
   - Hsieh’s “Hedge Fund Risk Factors” library states it provides the original seven hedge fund risk factors used to benchmark diversified hedge fund portfolios, including trend-following risk factors and equity/bond/emerging components [[77]](https://people.duke.edu/~dah7/HFRFData.htm). (The dataset here doesn’t include full factor construction details, so a framework should reference such libraries but require transparent publication of definitions.)

### D3) Why benchmark methodology must be stress-tested
The NBER working paper on benchmarking money manager performance shows abnormal returns and tracking errors can be highly sensitive to the benchmark methodology; sign disagreements across methods can be substantial in some periods [[72]](https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w12461/w12461.pdf). Therefore:
- The framework should require **benchmark robustness reporting**: alpha under a small set of *plausible benchmark models* (e.g., market-only; Fama-French style; style premia indices; constrained asset-mix regressions), and explicitly disclose sensitivity.

Deliverable: a “Benchmark Sensitivity Table” showing alpha/IR under each benchmark model, with consistent horizons and cost assumptions.

---

## 4) Adaptability to market conditions: how to define it and test it without fooling yourself

“Adaptability” must be separated into (i) *conditional exposure management* and (ii) *true edge preservation under distribution shift*, and must be tested in ways that are robust to overfitting.

### E1) Operational definition (make it measurable)
A strategy is “adaptive” if it satisfies **both**:

1) **Conditional performance stability**: risk-adjusted returns remain acceptable across regimes (vol regimes, liquidity regimes, macro regimes).  
2) **Controlled behavior change**: when exposures change, they do so for explainable reasons and without hidden risk loading.

### E2) Regime-conditional evaluation (standard requirement)
At minimum, slice performance by:
- Volatility regimes (low/medium/high)
- Trend regimes (up/down/sideways)
- Liquidity/spread regimes (wide spreads, high impact)
- Macro stress regimes based on standardized scenario variables (e.g., Fed severely adverse) [[93]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/publications/2025-stress-test-scenarios.htm)

Then report:
- Conditional Sharpe, drawdowns, ES, turnover, and capacity utilization.
- Regime transition performance (what happens when regime flips).

### E3) Time-varying exposure and attribution (to separate alpha vs moving betas)
A core problem is that factor loadings vary over time; standard rolling regressions can update slowly and overly smooth coefficients, misleading attribution when managers adjust exposures dynamically [[43]](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/financial-analysts-journal/2017/estimating-time-varying-factor-exposures). Holdings/characteristics-based estimation offers a way to build *dynamic and investable* factor benchmarks [[43]](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/financial-analysts-journal/2017/estimating-time-varying-factor-exposures).

Framework requirement:
- Provide both **return-based** and **holdings/position-based** exposure estimates (when positions are available).
- Report “alpha after time-varying exposures” and compare to “alpha under static exposures.”

### E4) Continual learning / distribution shift tests (ML-specific)
QuantBench (2025) explicitly argues there is a lack of standardized benchmarks aligned with industry practice for AI methods and presents an end‑to‑end benchmark platform; its empirical findings emphasize distribution shift can cause rapid model degradation and highlights the need for continual learning to address shifts [[98]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.18600.pdf).  

So, for ML strategies:
- Run **prequential (online) evaluation**: measure performance decay as a function of time since last retraining.
- Evaluate retraining rules under walk-forward / rolling deployments (Layer F).
- Require explicit reporting of “model degradation curves” and “retraining benefit vs cost” curves.

### E5) Guardrails against “fake adaptability” (overfitting dressed up as regime logic)
Any adaptability claim increases degrees of freedom (more parameters, more conditional rules), so the bar for evidence rises:
- Use **Reality Check / SPA-style joint tests** when searching over many adaptive variants [[39]](https://www.ssc.wisc.edu/~bhansen/718/White2000.pdf), [[55]](https://homepage.ntu.edu.tw/~ckuan/pdf/Step-SPA-20090720.pdf).  
- Use **PBO** to quantify fragility of selecting an adaptive configuration [[54]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/backtest-prob.pdf).  
- Use **DSR** to deflate Sharpe claims given multiple testing and non-normality [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf).

---

## 5) Evaluation protocol (the rigor engine): from backtest to “evidence package”

### F1) Three evaluation “grades” (standardize realism levels)
Borrowing from the idea that platforms can vary simulation realism by scenario [[98]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.18600.pdf), standardize three grades:

**Grade 1 — Research backtest (fast, conservative)**
- Bar-based fills with conservative slippage.
- Full trial accounting + DSR + RC/SPA.

**Grade 2 — Execution-aware simulation**
- Transaction cost analysis informed by microstructure.
- For HFT, incorporate LOB dynamics; queue-reactive simulation is explicitly positioned as useful for simulators and transaction cost analysis [[61]](https://arxiv.org/abs/1312.0563).

**Grade 3 — Paper/live shadow trading**
- Production-like timestamps/clock sync, live market data feeds, and operational controls.

A strategy must be comparable within a grade; cross-grade comparisons require explicit caveats.

### F2) Multiple-testing control is non-optional
Standard requirements:
- Declare N trials and selection procedure (mandatory) [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf), [[54]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/backtest-prob.pdf).  
- Provide:
  - DSR [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf)
  - PBO (CSCV) [[54]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/backtest-prob.pdf)
  - Reality Check / SPA family p-values for “best of many” selection [[39]](https://www.ssc.wisc.edu/~bhansen/718/White2000.pdf), [[55]](https://homepage.ntu.edu.tw/~ckuan/pdf/Step-SPA-20090720.pdf)

### F3) Validation and monitoring should mirror model risk management standards
SR 11-7 is directly portable: treat each strategy (signal + portfolio construction + execution logic) as a “model,” requiring documentation, independent validation, ongoing monitoring, and outcomes analysis/backtesting on periods not used in development [[64]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/sr1107.htm). SR 11-7 explicitly expects:
- Conceptual soundness review
- Ongoing monitoring (including benchmarking)
- Outcomes analysis (back-testing) aligned to the model horizon [[64]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/sr1107.htm)

Deliverable: a **Model/Strategy Validation Report** with:
- Purpose + intended use
- Assumptions/limitations
- Data quality assessment
- Implementation verification
- Monitoring thresholds and escalation rules

### F4) Supervisory-control expectations for automated trading (operational rigor)
FINRA emphasizes that testing prior to production is “essential,” and highlights risk assessment, code development/implementation controls, post-change review, and compliance communication with developers as effective supervisory practices for algorithmic trading [[63]](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/key-topics/algorithmic-trading). Even if not a law-like standard for all entities, it provides a practical baseline for what “rigorous evaluation” must include.

---

## 6) Execution, transaction costs, market impact, and capacity: standardizing the “net reality”

This is where many quant comparisons fail in practice, especially across HFT vs slower strategies.

### G1) Require a “cost stack” disclosure
At minimum:
- Explicit transaction cost model and calibration window
- Fees, spread, slippage, commissions
- Financing/borrow costs
- Market impact assumptions

The importance of microstructure realism is supported by:
- Cartea–Jaimungal–Penalva’s coverage of LOB, trading costs, market depth, price impact, cancellations, hidden orders, latency/tick size [[59]](https://assets.cambridge.org/97811070/91146/frontmatter/9781107091146_frontmatter.pdf)
- Hasbrouck’s framework modeling transaction price as efficient price + noise and the centrality of LOB market structure [[60]](https://www.acsu.buffalo.edu/~keechung/MGF743/Readings/Hasbrouck's%20book.pdf)
- Queue-reactive LOB simulation for TCA and complex algorithm evaluation [[61]](https://arxiv.org/abs/1312.0563)

### G2) Capacity analysis as a first-class metric
Even without a single authoritative “capacity formula” in the gathered sources, the framework can standardize *evidence*:
- Capacity vs expected shortfall in returns (PnL degradation curve as size increases)
- Impact model stress: widen spreads, reduce depth, increase volatility
- “Crowding stress” (impact multiplier)

For HFT, capacity must be expressed in terms of:
- Participation rate limits
- Queue share assumptions
- Latency budget sensitivity

---

## 7) AI/ML governance and adaptiveness after deployment (what standardized evaluation must add)

### H1) Inventory, risk rating, and lifecycle controls
SR 11-7 requires model inventories and periodic review [[64]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/sr1107.htm). A 2024 PwC survey reports inventory is a “standard market practice” and provides best-practice inventory fields: model description, roles, risk tiering, lifecycle info, findings, documents [[90]](https://www.pwc.com/cz/cs/temata/MRM-Survey-Report-2024.pdf). These map cleanly onto quant strategies.

### H2) AI-specific expectations: bias, explainability, monitoring
FINRA’s AI considerations recommend updating model risk management for ML complexity, including:
- Input data bias review
- Algorithm/parameter checks (risk thresholds)
- Output explainability
- Upfront and ongoing testing including stressed scenarios/new datasets
- Parallel runs before retirement of prior models
- Inventory of AI models with risk ratings
- Benchmarks such as false negatives and ongoing monitoring [[91]](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/key-topics/fintech/report/artificial-intelligence-in-the-securities-industry/key-challenges)

IOSCO’s AI report catalogs regulatory responses and notes governance, disclosure, outsourcing/vendor management, and lifecycle oversight are key themes across jurisdictions [[92]](https://www.iosco.org/library/pubdocs/pdf/IOSCOPD788.pdf). It also notes EU AI Act definition includes systems that “may exhibit adaptiveness after deployment” [[90]](https://www.pwc.com/cz/cs/temata/MRM-Survey-Report-2024.pdf), reinforcing that evaluation must cover post-deployment drift and controls.

Deliverable: an **AI Strategy Control Appendix** including:
- Explainability artifacts (feature importance summaries where possible)
- Drift monitoring metrics and triggers
- Human oversight and guardrails for autonomous actions (FINRA highlights thresholds/limits for autonomous behavior) [[91]](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/key-topics/fintech/report/artificial-intelligence-in-the-securities-industry/key-challenges)

---

## 8) Packaging the framework: a standardized “Quant Strategy Scorecard + Evidence Bundle”

A workable standard needs both a *human-readable tearsheet* and an *auditable evidence bundle*.

### I1) The tearsheet (summary)
Open-source ecosystems already generate tear sheets (e.g., QuantStats provides report generation and example tear sheets) [[96]](https://github.com/ranaroussi/quantstats), [[97]](https://assets.super.so/37c35fc7-65f9-41ba-8ec1-78a1ab0e2d3c/files/7fadd658-8ffb-464e-8457-7f813528e448.pdf). However, typical tear sheets omit the critical scientific controls (trial counts, DSR/PBO/RC, data lineage). So extend them.

**Required sections (standard order):**
1) Strategy specification (Layer A)
2) Backtest lineage card (Layer B)
3) Performance & risk table (Layer C)
4) Tail/drawdown page (VaR/ES, max drawdown) [[21]](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/v/var.asp), [[22]](https://www.mathematik.hu-berlin.de/~romisch/SP01/Uryasev.pdf), [[23]](https://ryanoconnellfinance.com/maximum-drawdown/)
5) Benchmark stack attribution (Layer D) per GIPS-style benchmark discipline [[70]](https://www.gipsstandards.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gs_benchmarks_firms.pdf)
6) Regime-conditional performance (Layer E)
7) Costs/capacity page (Layer G)
8) Statistical credibility page:
   - N trials disclosure [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf), [[54]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/backtest-prob.pdf)
   - DSR [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf)
   - PBO [[54]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/backtest-prob.pdf)
   - Reality Check / SPA results [[39]](https://www.ssc.wisc.edu/~bhansen/718/White2000.pdf), [[55]](https://homepage.ntu.edu.tw/~ckuan/pdf/Step-SPA-20090720.pdf)
9) Stress scenario page (Fed scenario library mapping) [[93]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/publications/2025-stress-test-scenarios.htm)
10) Governance/validation summary aligned to SR 11-7 [[64]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/sr1107.htm)

### I2) The evidence bundle (audit-ready)
Borrow SR 11-7 governance requirements: documentation sufficient for unfamiliar parties, validation artifacts, periodic reviews, outcomes analysis, and inventory tracking [[64]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/sr1107.htm). Incorporate PwC’s inventory fields (roles, lifecycle, findings, documents) [[90]](https://www.pwc.com/cz/cs/temata/MRM-Survey-Report-2024.pdf). Include:
- Code version hashes
- Parameter sets
- Full list of trials and selection rule
- Raw logs of simulated orders/fills (for execution-aware grades)
- Validation sign-offs and monitoring thresholds

---

## 9) How to aggregate / compare strategies without collapsing everything into one misleading rank

Because the evaluation is multi-dimensional, comparisons should be expressed as:

1) **Pareto frontiers** (return vs drawdown; return vs ES; return vs capacity; alpha vs robustness).  
2) **Constraint-based screening** (e.g., “ES(97.5%, 10d) < X, max DD < Y, PBO < z”).  
3) **Contextual ranking**: benchmark-relative strategies ranked by information ratio vs chosen benchmark stack; benchmark-agnostic by absolute + tail constraints (aligning with GIPS “benchmark agnostic” reality and use of target return hurdles) [[70]](https://www.gipsstandards.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gs_benchmarks_firms.pdf).

To prevent gaming:
- Publish rankings only if the strategy passes minimum robustness: DSR above threshold and RC/SPA not rejected as data-snooped luck, and PBO below threshold [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf), [[39]](https://www.ssc.wisc.edu/~bhansen/718/White2000.pdf), [[54]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/backtest-prob.pdf), [[55]](https://homepage.ntu.edu.tw/~ckuan/pdf/Step-SPA-20090720.pdf).

---

## 10) Where existing standards help—and where they still fail

### What adapts well
- **GIPS benchmark discipline** (pre-specified, relevant, investable, disclosed; peer-group limitations) [[70]](https://www.gipsstandards.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gs_benchmarks_firms.pdf).  
- **Model risk management** (SR 11-7) as the backbone for documentation, validation, monitoring, and auditability [[64]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/sr1107.htm).  
- **Multiple-testing aware inference**: Reality Check [[39]](https://www.ssc.wisc.edu/~bhansen/718/White2000.pdf), SPA/stepwise SPA [[55]](https://homepage.ntu.edu.tw/~ckuan/pdf/Step-SPA-20090720.pdf), DSR [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf), PBO [[54]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/backtest-prob.pdf).  
- **Cross-asset style premia baselines**: explicit, volatility-scaled long–short composites (AQR) [[73]](https://www.aqr.com/-/media/AQR/Documents/Insights/Journal-Article/JOIM-Investing-With-Style.pdf); emerging ARP benchmark suites emphasizing tradability and consensus definitions [[74]](https://thehedgefundjournal.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/10/bfinance-Benchmarking-ARP-v1.pdf).  
- **Execution realism foundations**: LOB, price impact, latency are central topics in algorithmic trading design and should be central in evaluation [[59]](https://assets.cambridge.org/97811070/91146/frontmatter/9781107091146_frontmatter.pdf); queue-reactive LOB simulation provides a concrete path to realistic TCA [[61]](https://arxiv.org/abs/1312.0563).

### What remains hard / open
1) **A single canonical factor set for all assets/strategies**: benchmark sensitivity is real; measured alpha can change sign across benchmark methodologies [[72]](https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w12461/w12461.pdf). The framework must embrace *benchmark stacks and sensitivity analysis*, not promise one “true alpha.”  
2) **Capacity standardization**: market impact is venue/instrument/regime dependent; the framework can standardize evidence artifacts and stress procedures, but a universal closed-form capacity metric is not established in the provided sources.  
3) **HFT comparability**: differences in infrastructure and latency budgets can dominate outcomes; evaluation must be grade-specific and include operational controls (consistent with FINRA’s emphasis on testing and supervision) [[63]](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/key-topics/algorithmic-trading).  
4) **Adaptive ML**: distribution shift degrades models quickly (QuantBench highlights this) [[98]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.18600.pdf); governance must include drift monitoring and controlled retraining, but industry-wide standardized drift metrics are still evolving.

---

## 11) A concrete “minimum viable standard” (MVS) you could implement now

If you want something implementable across a wide range of advanced quant strategies within months (not years), define the standard as:

**MVS-1 (Protocol)**
- Fixed evaluation grades (research, execution-aware, shadow-live).
- Trial accounting + DSR + PBO + RC/SPA mandatory for any strategy produced by specification search [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf), [[39]](https://www.ssc.wisc.edu/~bhansen/718/White2000.pdf), [[54]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/backtest-prob.pdf), [[55]](https://homepage.ntu.edu.tw/~ckuan/pdf/Step-SPA-20090720.pdf).
- SR 11-7 style validation and annual review cycle for any strategy in production [[64]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/sr1107.htm).

**MVS-2 (Benchmarks)**
- GIPS-style benchmark selection rules and disclosures [[70]](https://www.gipsstandards.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gs_benchmarks_firms.pdf).
- Benchmark stack: funding + market beta + cross-asset style premia (Value/Momentum/Carry/Defensive-style composites) [[73]](https://www.aqr.com/-/media/AQR/Documents/Insights/Journal-Article/JOIM-Investing-With-Style.pdf) + optional ARP suite alignment where relevant [[74]](https://thehedgefundjournal.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/10/bfinance-Benchmarking-ARP-v1.pdf).
- Benchmark sensitivity table required [[72]](https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w12461/w12461.pdf).

**MVS-3 (Metrics)**
- Must report: TWR/MWR aligned benchmark returns [[70]](https://www.gipsstandards.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gs_benchmarks_firms.pdf); Sharpe [[4]](http://www.stat.ucla.edu/~nchristo/statistics_c183_c283/sharpe__mutual_fund_performance.pdf); VaR [[21]](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/v/var.asp); CVaR/ES [[22]](https://www.mathematik.hu-berlin.de/~romisch/SP01/Uryasev.pdf); max drawdown [[23]](https://ryanoconnellfinance.com/maximum-drawdown/); skew/kurtosis [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf); autocorrelation diagnostics [[28]](http://www.efmaefm.org/0EFMAMEETINGS/EFMA%20ANNUAL%20MEETINGS/2024-Lisbon/papers/OFM.pdf); scenario stress results mapped to Fed variables where applicable [[93]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/publications/2025-stress-test-scenarios.htm).

**MVS-4 (Reporting & audit)**
- Standard tear sheet + evidence bundle.
- Model/strategy inventory fields based on SR 11-7 expectations [[64]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/sr1107.htm) and best-practice inventory structure summarized by PwC [[90]](https://www.pwc.com/cz/cs/temata/MRM-Survey-Report-2024.pdf).
- For AI strategies: FINRA AI MRM enhancements (bias review, explainability artifacts, drift tests, parallel runs, monitoring benchmarks) [[91]](https://www.finra.org/rules-guidance/key-topics/fintech/report/artificial-intelligence-in-the-securities-industry/key-challenges), with regulatory-context awareness per IOSCO mapping [[92]](https://www.iosco.org/library/pubdocs/pdf/IOSCOPD788.pdf).

---

## 12) Bottom line: yes—build a general yet rigorous framework by standardizing *process + benchmark stack + disclosure*, not by searching for one benchmark

A credible universal comparison framework is achievable if it:
- Treats strategies as models and adopts SR 11-7-grade governance, validation, and monitoring [[64]](https://www.federalreserve.gov/supervisionreg/srletters/sr1107.htm).
- Uses GIPS-grade benchmark discipline and disclosures [[70]](https://www.gipsstandards.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/gs_benchmarks_firms.pdf).
- Makes multiple testing and overfitting controls first-class (Reality Check / SPA, DSR, PBO) [[37]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/deflated-sharpe.pdf), [[39]](https://www.ssc.wisc.edu/~bhansen/718/White2000.pdf), [[54]](https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbpapers/backtest-prob.pdf), [[55]](https://homepage.ntu.edu.tw/~ckuan/pdf/Step-SPA-20090720.pdf).
- Standardizes execution realism tiers grounded in microstructure and LOB simulation where needed [[59]](https://assets.cambridge.org/97811070/91146/frontmatter/9781107091146_frontmatter.pdf), [[60]](https://www.acsu.buffalo.edu/~keechung/MGF743/Readings/Hasbrouck's%20book.pdf), [[61]](https://arxiv.org/abs/1312.0563).
- Quantifies adaptability via regime-conditional evaluation, time-varying exposure attribution, and distribution-shift testing (supported by time-varying factor exposure challenges [[43]](https://rpc.cfainstitute.org/research/financial-analysts-journal/2017/estimating-time-varying-factor-exposures) and QuantBench’s focus on shifts/continual learning) [[98]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.18600.pdf).
- Publishes everything as a repeatable, auditable scorecard + evidence bundle.

If you implement those elements, you won’t get a single “one-number benchmark,” but you *will* get accurate, defensible, and comparable evaluation across multi-factor, HFT, and ML strategies—while making it much harder to game results and much easier to diagnose why strategies differ.
